perturbation.original_text: The term “Confidential Information” shall include all Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that concerns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other proprietary business information.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor agrees he/she will not remove any document, equipment or other materials from the premises without Hydranautics’ written consent. Visitor will not photograph, videotape, or otherwise make any record of or preserve any Confidential Information to which he/she may be given access during the facility tour(s).
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause that concerns 'removal of materials from the premises without Hydranautics' written consent' and placing in it's location the definition of confidential information hides the definition of confidential information. This makes it harder to enforce the agreement as to which data it covers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The definition of trade secrets is essential under CUTSA. Obscuring the definition of 'Confidential Information' which includes trade secrets affects the ability to prove what information is actually protected under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification obscures the definition of 'Confidential Information,' which, according to the Law Explanation, is essential for proving what information is protected under CUTSA (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act). Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites CIV 3426, the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, indicating the legal importance of trade secrets definition. By moving the definition, the changed text makes it more difficult to enforce the agreement regarding what data it covers, thus contradicting the intent of CUTSA to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor will abide by all safety regulations, stay within designated areas of the facility per the established tour, and will not leave the touring party without an escort by a Hydranautics authorized employee.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Shifting the governing law to the 'safety regulations' section buries the legal framework of the agreement within operational details. It's customary to have it in the initial or concluding sections for clarity. The location of the safety agreement is unusual.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1859
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1859 states that if general and particular provisions are inconsistent, the latter is paramount. Placing the governing law within the section on safety regulations may imply that the legal framework is subordinate to safety procedures, which could cause confusion in interpretation. It makes it less visible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1859 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1859. In the construction of a statute the intention of the Legislature, and in the construction of the instrument the intention of the parties, is to be pursued, if possible; and when a general and particular provision are inconsistent, the latter is paramount to the former. So a particular intent will control a general one that is inconsistent with it. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original safety regulations with a clause regarding governing law, specifically stating that the agreement is construed in accordance with California law. The perturbation explanation states that placing the governing law within the section on safety regulations is unusual, and makes it less visible. The Law Explanation adds that Section 1859 of the California Code of Civil Procedure indicates that if general and particular provisions are inconsistent, the latter is paramount. The original placement of the safety regulations alongside other operational details makes the overall governing law of the contract less clear. The modified text introduces a structural flaw by embedding the legal framework within a section on what should have been safety protocols.
